title: EFL Awards 2026: The managers driving their teams to glory this season
text: The 2025/26 EFL season has reached its climax across the Championship, League One, and League Two divisions, with the awards ceremony set for April 19, 2026. Holly Wright examines the managers nominated for Manager of the Year honors. Championship Manager of the Year: Frank Lampard's Coventry City has emerged as a genuine title contender under his management. The Sky Blues employ a dynamic attacking style, complemented by disciplined defensive structures that make them hard to beat, positioning them for potential championship promotion. Alex Neil has kept Millwall consistent throughout the campaign. His tactical discipline and defensive organization have enabled the Lions to remain firmly in automatic promotion contention while maintaining attacking threat on transitions. Kim Hellberg reinvigorated Middlesbrough mid-season, providing the club renewed belief in promotion ambitions through tactical flexibility and structured attacking play. Sergej Jakirovic has transformed Hull City into a competitive play-off contender despite resource constraints, maximizing performance through morale management and tactical adaptation. League One Manager of the Year: Michael Skubala's Lincoln City achievement stands out dramatically. The Imps secured promotion on Easter Monday and maintain a 12-point lead over Cardiff City while on a 24-game unbeaten run, blending attacking fluidity with defensive organisation. Brian Barry-Murphy has Cardiff City positioned second, maintaining consistency and focus to secure seven-point buffer over third place. Graham Alexander keeps Bradford City in third place through tactical organization and motivational leadership, keeping promotion hopes alive. Alex Revell has guided Stevenage to a late-season surge into the final play-off spot through tactical adjustments and motivational skill. League Two Manager of the Year: Andy Woodman's Bromley leads the division by seven points through tactical balance combining attacking flair with defensive discipline. Paul Warne keeps MK Dons competitive in second place on goal difference through tactical adaptability and high-pressure motivation. Micky Mellon maintains Oldham Athletic's competitiveness through consistent performances emphasizing defensive organization with selective attacking risk. Neil Harris guides Cambridge United toward play-off contention through tactical discipline and squad rotation management.
